perturbation.original_text: 1.0 Definitions a. "Acceptable Use Policy" means the Acceptable Use Policy for IBM e-business Services, located on the Internet at http://www.ibm.com/services/ e-business/aup.html, as of the Effective Date, and any subsequent modification in accordance with Section 13.2 below.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.0 Definitions a. "Acceptable Use Policy" means a document outlining guidelines for using IBM e-business Services, subject to change.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition provided a specific URL for the Acceptable Use Policy. The modification removes the URL, creating ambiguity about where to find the policy. This ambiguity leads to a contradiction in Section 11.2(b), which states that Customer's use of Services and all Content will comply with the Acceptable Use Policy; if the Acceptable Use Policy location is undefined, compliance cannot be verifiably enforced, and makes Section 11.2.b contradictory.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11.2 Representations and Warranties Customer represents and warrants that: a. it has the requisite corporate power and authority to execute, deliver and perform its obligations under this Agreement; Customer has no contractual or other obligation that (i) restricts or prohibits Customer's execution or performance of this Agreement, or (ii) Customer will breach in connection with the execution or performance of this Agreement; and b. its use of the Services and all Content will comply with the Acceptable Use Policy.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines the 'Acceptable Use Policy' with a specific URL. The changed text removes this URL, making it ambiguous where the policy is located. Section 11.2(b) requires the customer to comply with the Acceptable Use Policy. Without a defined location for the policy, compliance cannot be verifiably enforced, creating a contradiction. The removal of the URL directly undermines the enforceability of Section 11.2(b).

perturbation.original_text: 8.1 Indemnification by IBM If a third party claims or threatens a claim that Materials or Base Components IBM provides to Customer or uses in connection with the performance of the Services infringe that party's patent, trademark, copyright, or trade secret, then IBM will indemnify, defend and hold harmless the Customer, its Enterprise and their respective employees, officers, agents and directors against that claim or threatened claim at IBM's expense and pay all costs, damages, penalties and reasonable attorneys' fees that a court finally awards in connection with that claim (or which IBM&sbsp;agrees in any final settlement) provided that Customer: a. promptly notifies IBM in writing of the claim; and b. allows IBM to control, and cooperates with IBM in, the defense and any related settlement negotiations (it being understood and agreed that if Customer incurs any costs in connection with such cooperation, over and above nominal costs, IBM shall reimburse Customer therefor, such costs to potentially include, without limitation, the costs incurred by Customer in connection with depositions, responses to interrogatories, or testimony at trial or any similar proceeding, and travel costs in connection therewith; provided, however, such obligation of IBM as set forth in this parenthetical is conditioned upon Customer first obtaining IBM's consent to the incurring of such costs, such consent to be reasonably granted).
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 Indemnification by IBM If a third party claims or threatens a claim that Materials or Base Components IBM provides to Customer or uses in connection with the performance of the Services infringe that party's patent, trademark, copyright, or trade secret, then IBM will indemnify, defend and hold harmless the Customer, its Enterprise and their respective employees, officers, agents and directors against that claim or threatened claim at IBM's expense and pay all costs, damages, penalties and reasonable attorneys' fees that a court finally awards in connection with that claim (or which IBM&sbsp;agrees in any final settlement) provided that Customer: a. promptly notifies IBM in writing of the claim; and b. allows IBM to control, and cooperates with IBM in, the defense and any related settlement negotiations.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the clause pertaining to IBM reimbursing Customer's cooperation costs during legal defense in an indemnification claim. This creates a contradiction because the initial part of Section 8.1 states IBM will indemnify and defend, implying complete financial coverage related to the defense. Removing the cooperation cost clause introduces uncertainty as to the extent of IBM's financial responsibility, especially if the Customers incur high costs. This creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.1 Indemnification by IBM If a third party claims or threatens a claim that Materials or Base Components IBM provides to Customer or uses in connection with the performance of the Services infringe that party's patent, trademark, copyright, or trade secret, then IBM will indemnify, defend and hold harmless the Customer, its Enterprise and their respective employees, officers, agents and directors against that claim or threatened claim at IBM's expense and pay all costs, damages, penalties and reasonable attorneys' fees that a court finally awards in connection with that claim (or which IBM&sbsp;agrees in any final settlement) provided that Customer: a. promptly notifies IBM in writing of the claim; and b. allows IBM to control, and cooperates with IBM in, the defense and any related settlement negotiations.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit clause in Section 8.1 obligating IBM to reimburse the Customer for cooperation costs (depositions, interrogatories, trial testimony, travel) during legal defense. This directly contradicts the initial part of Section 8.1, which states IBM will 'indemnify, defend and hold harmless the Customer... at IBM's expense'. By removing the clause that clarifies the scope of 'at IBM's expense,' the modified text creates ambiguity and a potential contradiction regarding IBM's financial responsibility for the Customer's legal defense costs related to cooperation, which were originally covered, leading to an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2 Renewal Each Service Option Attachment will renew automatically for an additional term equal in duration to the previous term of the applicable Service Option Attachment unless either party notifies the other party in writing at least ninety (90) days prior to the end of the then-current term for the applicable Service Option Attachment that it has elected to terminate such Service Option Attachment.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 Renewal Each Service Option Attachment may renew automatically. Details are outlined in individual service agreements.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific 90-day notice requirement for termination of Service Option Attachments creates ambiguity. While the changed text allows for automatic renewal, it removes the clarity of the original clause about the notice period required to prevent it. Because of the omition in the clause, the text is now creating a contradiction with the Section 3.1 where it specifies the term, because now the term is up to chance if the renewal is automatic or not.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Term This Agreement will be effective beginning on 12:01 a.m., Eastern Time, on the day after the date of last signature to these Base Terms ("Effective Date") and ending on the expiration and/or termination of all Service Option Attachments, unless the Agreement is terminated earlier in accordance with the terms herein. The term of each Service Option Attachment is as specified on the applicable Order Form.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 3.2 removes the specific 90-day notice requirement for termination of Service Option Attachments. This omission creates a contradiction with Section 3.1, which states that the agreement ends on the expiration/termination of all Service Option Attachments, with the term specified on the order form. Without the 90-day notice, it's unclear how termination occurs consistently, creating ambiguity and conflicting with the defined term in 3.1.
